coredata.dc:title: Enhancing specific capacitance and cycling stability of zinc oxide-based supercapacitors using gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Phetcharee, Kulpriya
Pholauyphon, Wasinee
Kwamman, Tanagorn
Sirisit, Natee
Manyam, Jedsada
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Due to an energy crisis, the development of low-cost, high-performance supercapacitors is of great interest. This paper describes for the first time the use of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated carbon dots to improve the ZnO-based supercapacitor performance. The carbon dots were irradiated by gamma ray at 50 kGy (CDs-EDA-50) and 200 kGy (CDs-EDA-200) in the presence of ethylenediamine (EDA). Compared to the pure ZnO electrode, the ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes boosted the specific capacitance by 194% and 312%, respectively. In addition to the specific capacitance, the cycling stability was also improved from 87.41% to 99.87% after 8500 cycles. Through several electrochemical analyses and contact angle measurement, the modified carbon dots in the composite electrodes were found to enhance several key factors, including surface-controlled capacitance, series resistance, ion diffusion coefficient, electrochemical active surface area, wettability, and mechanical integrity of the electrode, resulting in higher specific capacitance and cycling stability. This work demonstrates that the use of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated carbon dots as electrode additives has potential for ZnO-based supercapacitors and can be applied for the fabrication of other high-performance energy storage devices.

head.ce:title: Enhancing specific capacitance and cycling stability of zinc oxide-based supercapacitors using gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to an energy crisis, the development of low-cost, high-performance supercapacitors is of great interest. This paper describes for the first time the use of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated carbon dots to improve the ZnO-based supercapacitor performance. The carbon dots were irradiated by gamma ray at 50 kGy (CDs-EDA-50) and 200 kGy (CDs-EDA-200) in the presence of ethylenediamine (EDA). Compared to the pure ZnO electrode, the ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes boosted the specific capacitance by 194% and 312%, respectively. In addition to the specific capacitance, the cycling stability was also improved from 87.41% to 99.87% after 8500 cycles. Through several electrochemical analyses and contact angle measurement, the modified carbon dots in the composite electrodes were found to enhance several key factors, including surface-controlled capacitance, series resistance, ion diffusion coefficient, electrochemical active surface area, wettability, and mechanical integrity of the electrode, resulting in higher specific capacitance and cycling stability. This work demonstrates that the use of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated carbon dots as electrode additives has potential for ZnO-based supercapacitors and can be applied for the fabrication of other high-performance energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The demand for fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, and natural gas) has continuously risen with the fast growth and development of the global economy. According to the statistical report [1], the global demand for total equivalent oil was estimated to be over 13.7 billion tons in 2012 and will rise to around 18 billion tons by 2035, causing fossil fuel depletion and global warming. These situations have already caused economic and environmental crises in price fluctuations and unbalanced supply chains, resulting in intense research activity for the exploitation of environmentally friendly and limitless energy sources. Therefore, developing both sustainable energy supplies and energy storage technologies can be achieved to overcome these problems [2]. Among several energy storage devices, supercapacitors are the promising candidates due to their excellent properties in terms of high power density, rapid charging/discharging rate, and long cycling stability [3]. However, their applications are limited by the lack of appropriate electrodes with excellent electrochemical performance [4,5]. The electrode materials of supercapacitors are divided into two types. The first type is carbon materials with a high surface area, such as activated carbon [6], reduced graphene oxide [7], and carbon aerogel [8], providing a large surface area and electrostatic interactions. The second type is metal oxides and conducting polymers, which are pseudocapacitive materials, as they can improve the electrochemical capacity of electrodes via redox reactions or Faradaic processes [9]. Therefore, a hybrid between carbon-based materials and metal oxides has been receiving attention for supercapacitors, which are deemed to generate faradic capacitance and other effects to upgrade the performances [10].

Various metal oxides, such as ruthenium oxide (RuO2), nickel oxide (NiO2), and manganese oxide (MnO2), have been widely used as composite electrode materials for supercapacitors [11–13]. Even though these metal oxide-based electrodes provide high specific capacitance and good electrochemical properties, their cost and toxicity are too high when compared to other inexpensive and less toxic metal oxides, thus, limiting their commercial applications. On the other hand, low-cost, environmentally friendly zinc oxide (ZnO) is widely utilized as a catalyst [14], UV absorber [15], in solar cells [16], in batteries [17], etc. Furthermore, due to its wide bandgap, high stability, good electrical properties, and ease of synthesis, ZnO is a promising candidate for supercapacitors [18]. Various techniques, such as the sol-gel method [19], hydrothermal treatment [20], and microwave-assisted synthesis [21], can be used to prepare ZnO in various morphologies, such as nanorods [22], nanocones [23], nanowires [23], and nanoparticles [22]. Among these techniques, the sol-gel method is interesting because it is inexpensive and straightforward to control the formation of ZnO nanoparticles [24]. However, ZnO-based supercapacitors remain limited by their low rate capacitance because of a slow faradic reaction and low electron transportability at high current [25,26]. Alver et al. reported the ZnO nanoparticle-based supercapacitors prepared from three different precursors and obtained merely as high as 5.87 F g−1 [27]. To improve the electrochemical performance, the fabrication of composite electrodes between ZnO nanoparticles and other materials can synergistically offer positive results for supercapacitors.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials, the majority of them reported with sizes less than 20 nm [28]. They are composed of sp2 and sp3 hybridizations as well as hydrophilic functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups on their surface [29]. They have been widely used in various applications, such as bio-imaging, sensing, drug delivery, and photocatalysis [30]. Because of their unique electrical characteristics, they have also been used in energy storage applications. For example, Lv et al. fabricated carbon dot-induced MnO2 nanowires as electrode materials. The composite electrodes exhibited an enhanced specific capacitance of 340 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, compared to 232 F g–1 for the electrode without the CDs. They claimed that the CDs increased the electrode-electrolyte wettability, hence improving the charge transfer, ion migration kinetics, and electrolyte ion transport and storage in porous electrodes [31]. Zhang et al. prepared the graphene oxide/CDs/polypyrrole composite electrode. It exhibited a high specific capacitance of 576 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, whereas the specific capacitance of the electrode in the absence of CDs was 468 F g−1 [32]. Also, Zhao et al. reported that the CDs acted as pseudo-capacitive materials, promoting the redox reaction necessary for enhancing the electrochemical performance of the electrode [33]. Based on these studies, CDs were demonstrated to be potent performance enhancers in supercapacitors and have the potential for ZnO nanoparticle-based supercapacitors. Recently, Sinha et al. fabricated the ZnO/CDs/SWCNT composite electrodes for supercapacitors, but a specific capacitance of only 1.53 mF cm−2 was obtained, which is still low for practical applications [34]. Fang et al. reported a specific capacitance as high as 786 F g−1 using a ZnO/graphene composite electrode, but graphene is more expensive and difficult to prepare on a large scale, compared to the CDs [35], Hence, the performance of CDs/ZnO nanoparticle-based supercapacitors must be greatly improved in order to compete with other types of supercapacitors.

Gamma irradiation is a form of electromagnetic radiation that emits photons from excited nuclei. The emitted photons provide high energy and short wavelengths in the electromagnetic radiation spectrum. Due to its high energy, gamma irradiation has been used in a variety of applications, such as radiotherapy, sterilization, microbial decontamination, and material modification [36]. Several phenomena, including structural modifications, bond breaking, free radical formation, cross-linking, and chain scission, occur when gamma rays interact with the materials [37]. Jovanović et al. reported that the irradiation dose of gamma rays affected the structure of graphene quantum dots and one of the interesting results was an improved singlet oxygen generation and photostability [38]. Jihao et al. prepared the gamma-irradiated graphene oxide in the presence of ethylenediamine (EDA). After irradiation, they observed the elimination of hydroxyl, epoxide, and carboxyl groups as well as the incorporation of nitrogen atoms in the structure of graphene oxide [39]. In another study, gamma irradiation in the presence of isopropyl alcohol and EDA was employed for the preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [40]. It was observed that the percentage of carbon atoms increased from 63% to more than 79%. Additionally, the zeta potential of the obtained graphene quantum dots changed from − 34.6 to + 9.1 mV, owing to the modification of functional groups localized on the surface, resulting in increased photoluminescence intensity. Moreover, it was also revealed that the gamma-irradiated graphene quantum dots emitted an intense blue light, compared to the graphene quantum dots without gamma irradiation treatment, which emitted violet to blue and evergreen light. Blue photoluminescence of gamma-irradiated graphene quantum dots resulted from the sp2 core, while green photoluminescence originated from the functional groups [41,42]. Based on these previous studies, gamma irradiation can effectively modify the carbon-based materials that can potentially improve the supercapacitor performance and thus needs further investigation.

Gamma-irradiated CDs have been applied in various applications, such as photocatalysts [36], sensors [40], medical treatment [43], and biological imaging [44]. However, they have never been employed for supercapacitors. Therefore, the goal of this research is to fabricate novel composite electrodes that consist of gamma-irradiated CDs and ZnO nanoparticles and to investigate their supercapacitive properties. The CDs were irradiated by gamma irradiation in the presence of EDA to install the ammonium groups on the surface of the CDs, and the ZnO nanoparticles were prepared by a straightforward sol-gel method. A combination of outstanding electrical characteristics of ZnO nanoparticles and unique surface properties of CDs is expected to synergistically improve the electrode's electrochemical performance for supercapacitor. The effects of gamma irradiation dose and amine passivation on the electrochemical performance were investigated. Several electrochemical analyses were performed to elucidate the effects of the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs, which provide a body of knowledge necessary for further development of high-performance supercapacitors. Based on this work, we have demonstrated a novel strategy for modifying CDs using gamma irradiation and amine passivation for supercapacitor applications, which are the future of emerging energy storage systems.

Polyurethane foam was purchased from Uratex, Philippines. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich unless otherwise noted. Sulfuric acid (96 wt%) was obtained from CARLO ERBA Reagents. All reagents were used without further purification. High-grade regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) was purchased from Membrane Filtration Product, Inc. Deionized (DI) water (15 MΩ) was used to prepare solutions in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles by sol-gel method
ce:section.ce:para:
Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)22H2O) (20 g) was dissolved in 150 mL DI water and stirred for 20 min at room temperature to form a homogeneous zinc acetate solution. Sodium hydroxide (80 g) was dissolved in 80 mL DI water and stirred for 20 min at room temperature. The prepared zinc acetate and sodium hydroxide solutions were then mixed. After mixing, 100 mL ethanol was added dropwise into the mixture with vigorous stirring for 90 min to obtain a gel-like product. After the stirring was stopped, a white precipitate was formed. The precipitate was filtered and washed with DI water and absolute ethanol several times and then dried at 80 °C for 4 h. The obtained powder was calcined at 400 °C for 2 h in a furnace. Finally, ZnO nanoparticles were obtained. The chemical reactions for the formation of ZnO nanoparticles are shown in Eqs. 1 and 2 [45].
(1) Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O + 2NaOH → Zn(OH)2 + 2CH3COONa + 2H2O
(2) Zn(OH)2 → ZnO + H2O

The synthesis of CDs was performed following our previous work where characterizations can be found [46]. In brief, polyurethane foam (5 g) was mixed with 50 mL of 2.5 M sulfuric acid solution in a porcelain crucible and pyrolyzed at 250 °C for 4 h in a furnace. After the reaction, a black solid was cooled to room temperature and added with 50 mL DI water to remove the CDs from the residue. The resulting mixture was then placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min to ensure the transfer of CDs from the surface of the carbonized polyurethane to water. The mixture was filtered to obtain the resultant CDs solution, which was then neutralized by sodium hydroxide and dialyzed for 4 days to remove impurities. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the CDs powder.

CDs (0.1 g) were mixed with 4 mL EDA, 3 mL isopropyl alcohol, and 93 mL DI water. Before irradiation, the prepared solution was ultrasonically agitated while bubbling with nitrogen gas to remove oxygen molecules from the dispersion. The prepared solution was irradiated using an irradiation unit with Co-60 as an irradiation source (Gamma chamber 5000, BRIT, India) with a dose rate of 2.00 kGy h−1 and a dosage of 50 and 200 kGy. After irradiation, the obtained solution was then added with sodium hydroxide, dialyzed, and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs powder. The obtained powders irradiated with a dose of 50 and 200 kGy in the presence of EDA were labeled CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200, respectively. For the preparation of gamma-irradiated CDs in this case without the addition of EDA, the CDs (0.1 g) were mixed with 3 mL isopropyl alcohol and 97 mL DI water. The remaining experimental procedure was the same as previously mentioned. The gamma-irradiated samples with a dose of 50 and 200 kGy were denoted as CDs-50 and CDs-200, respectively Scheme 1.

The obtained ZnO nanoparticles were mixed with carbon black and poly(vinylidene fluoride) as a binder in a ratio of 80:15:5% w/w in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone as a solvent to form a slurry. To investigate the effects of gamma-irradiated CDs on the electrochemical performance, CDs-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 (5% w/w each) were added to the slurry, which were denoted as ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200, respectively. The addition of unmodified CDs (5% w/w) to the electrode was used as a control sample, which was denoted as ZnO-CDs. The slurry was coated on carbon paper as a current collector and then dried at 80 °C in the oven for 2 h to obtain the electrode. The mass loading of the electrode is 2.4 mg.

To prepare the electrolytes, 5.6, 16.8, and 33.7 g of potassium hydroxide were added into 100 mL DI water to prepare the KOH solutions at the concentrations of 1, 3, and 6 M, respectively. The prepared electrodes were immersed in the KOH electrolytes for 1 h prior to characterization.

The functional groups on the surface and elemental components of CDs were characterized using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2000) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratosanalytical, Manchester UK). The morphology and particle size of CDs were determined using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100). The accelerating voltage was 200 kV. The optical properties of CDs were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec) and a fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, FP-6200). The morphology and structure of ZnO nanoparticles were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (JEOL, JEM-2010) and an X-ray diffractometer (XRD; Rigaku, SmartLab). The CDs, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 electrodes were also examined using a Raman spectrometer (NT-MDT, NTEGRASpectra model). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-200 electrodes were measured using Microtrac MRB BELSORP MAX. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was calculated according to the BET model, while the pore volume and pore diameter were determined using the Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. Contact angle analysis was conducted using a Biolin Scientific contact angle meter and electrolyte droplet (1 µL).

Electrochemical cells in three- and two-electrode configurations were employed for electrochemical characterization using a potentiostat/galvanostat (μAutolab, PGSTAT204). In the three-electrode system, a Pt counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and the prepared electrode as a working electrode were used for the electrochemical response measurement using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at the scan rates of 5, 10, 50, 70, and 100 mV s−1 in a voltage window ranging from − 0.3–0.3 V for 3 cycles and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) which was conducted in a frequency range between 100 kHz and 100 mHz with an amplitude of 10 mV. The two-electrode configuration containing a pair of symmetric electrodes was used for galvanic charge-discharge (GCD) assay at current densities ranging from 0.3 to 2 A g−1. A Swagelok cell was used to fabricate the supercapacitor for the cycling stability test using Whatman filter paper with a diameter of 1 cm as a separator.

A specific capacitance (C
s , F g−1) was derived from the cyclic voltammogram and calculated by applying the following expression (Eq. 3):
(3)
C s
=
∫
IdV
2 mv Δ V
where I (A) is the current applied to the device, V (V) is the voltage, v (V s−1) is the scan rate, ΔV (V) is the window voltage, and m (g) is the mass of active materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ZnO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized from zinc acetate dihydrate and sodium hydroxide via the sol-gel method ( Fig. 1a). The SEM image of the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles is illustrated in Fig. 1b. The image shows the agglomeration of ZnO nanoparticles, which is similar to the previous report [47]. Furthermore, the crystalline structure and purity of the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles were confirmed by the XRD pattern. The XRD pattern of the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles was shown in Fig. 1c and compared with that of zincite (COD ID: 2300112) as a benchmark [48]. All the peaks from the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles matched well with those of zincite. Moreover, the XRD result from ZnO nanoparticles reveals no impurity peak, confirming the successful synthesis of ZnO nanoparticles with no detectable impurity from this process. To calculate the average diameter of the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles, the maximum intense (101) peak is utilized using the Debye-Scherrer formula [49], as shown in Eq. 4:
(4)
d =
k λ
β cos
θ
where d is the crystallite size of ZnO nanoparticles, k is the Scherrer constant, which is 0.9 for spherical particles, λ is the wavelength of X-ray (1.5418 Ǻ), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM), which is determined from the 2θ value of maximum intensity peak, and θ is the Bragg’s diffraction angle. The average crystallite size of the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles was calculated to be 22.45 nm. Again, this confirms that the ZnO nanoparticles were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs, CDs-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its high energy, gamma irradiation has been widely applied for material structural modification because it is a powerful technique. In this work, we chose this technique to modify CDs in the presence of EDA prepared from polyurethane and then used the modified CDs for the ZnO-based supercapacitors. Interestingly, the CDs after gamma treatment have never been employed for this application. First, the CDs were prepared from polyurethane and sulfuric acid, in which their characterization can be found in our previous publication [46]. Four different types of modified CDs were then prepared. CDs-50 and CDs-200 were prepared using 50 and 200 kGy in the absence of EDA, whereas CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 were synthesized using 50 and 200 kGy in the presence of EDA. After the synthesis, all CDs were purified using the dialysis method and freeze-dried to obtain the powders for further characterization and utilization.

The functional groups of all prepared CDs, including CDs, CDs-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 2a revealed the bands at 1610 cm−1 (CO/CN) and 1050 cm−1 (C-O/C-N) in CDs, CDs-50, CDs-200, CD-s-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200. The bands at 3200–2900 cm−1 (O-H/N-H/C-H), 1530 cm−1 (CC), and 825 cm−1 (N-H wagging) were detected only in CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 along with several sharp signals in the fingerprint region [46], but they were not observed in CDs, CDs-50, and CDs-200, confirming that EDA was incorporated into the CDs upon gamma irradiation and amine passivation. The appearance of CC signals indicates the formation of sp2 hybridized carbon after gamma irradiation and amine passivation, which was also in agreement with previous reports [41,42]. Comparing CDs-50 and CDs-200, the CC/CO/CN vibration signals of the CDs-200 are weaker, indicating the higher gamma irradiation dose might destroy these units. Furthermore, a signal at 1610 cm−1 was more apparent in CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200, which is an indicator of the imine formation after gamma irradiation/amine passivation [40]. Gamma irradiation in the absence of EDA (CDs-50 and CDs-200), on the other hand, did not yield a noticeable change to the FT-IR signals with respect to the original CDs. This means that the gamma irradiation without EDA did not effectively cause significant structural changes. All prepared CDs showed a C-S bond at 602 cm−1 because of the use of sulfuric acid as a reagent for the synthesis of CDs. The presence of nitrogen-containing groups and sp2 carbon confirms the successful gamma irradiation and amine passivation of CDs. The presence of polar functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, is responsible for their high solubility in water and polar organic solvents.

To gain more information about the gamma irradiation/amine passivation effects, XPS was also used to characterize all CDs. Fig. 2b depicts the survey XPS spectra showing four relevant signals of O1 s, N1 s, C1 s, and S2p at 532.2, 399.5, 285.0, and 168.1 eV, respectively. The nitrogen atomic concentrations of only CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 were above 17%, indicating that EDA was effectively incorporated into the CDs. The high-resolution O1 s spectra of all samples showed the presence of C-OH, C-O, CO, and OC-O (Fig. 2c) whereas the high-resolution N1 s spectra revealed the C-N-C, N-C3, and N-H groups (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution C1 s signal of all samples can be deconvoluted into four Gaussian peaks, which are CC/C-C, C-N, CO, and C-O (Fig. 2e) [50–53]. The more pronounced N-H peak at 401.3 eV and C-N peak at 286.0 eV with respect to the CC/C-C and the increase in nitrogen atomic concentration in the CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 indicated the successful amine passivation under gamma irradiation.

The morphology and particle size of all CDs were characterized by TEM ( Fig. 3a-e). The images show that all CDs were mostly spherical with an average diameter of 4.0, 4.0, 3.6, 24.8, and 29.8 nm, respectively (Fig. 3f-j). The CDs-200 has a slightly smaller size than the CDs-50, suggesting the higher gamma irradiation dose might degrade some of the surface functional groups. The surface passivation of EDA obviously led to larger nanoparticle size. Interestingly, the core-shell-like structure of CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 showed a darker carbon core and a lighter outer EDA layer. The thicknesses of the outer layers of CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 are 8.0 and 12.7 nm, and hence, a thicker passivating layer in CDs-EDA-200 than in CDs-EDA-50 confirms that the amount of passivation depends on the irradiation dose. The zeta potentials of CDs, CDs-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 were − 12, + 6.8, + 8.1, + 14.7, and + 3.2 mV, respectively. The positive zeta potential values after modification of CDs resulted from the re-polymerization of functional groups on CDs and the passivation of EDA also installed the positively charged ammonium groups.

The optical absorption and emission of all prepared CDs were measured using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV-Vis spectra of CDs, CDs-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 are shown in Fig. 4a-e. The shoulder absorption peak at 250 nm was assigned to the π-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 carbon while the absorption peak at around 300 nm arose from the n-π * transition [54]. The n-π * transition was apparent only in CDs, CDs-50, and CDs-200 whereas the π-π * transition was observed as a shoulder in CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200. Since the n-π * transition in CDs is generally due to the carbonyl groups, the carbonyl groups somewhat disappeared or reduced in the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs, again indicating the successful surface passivation by EDA on the CDs, possibly through the formation of imines, which is consistent with the FT-IR results. These results revealed that gamma irradiation and amine passivation caused a significant structural change in the CDs, leading to a change in UV–vis absorption. The highest fluorescence intensity of CDs, CD-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 was observed at 494, 489, 485, 483, and 477 nm, respectively, when using an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 4f-j). The gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs showed blue-shifted fluorescence emission as compared to the unmodified CDs, and it has been well known that the amine-passivated CDs dominantly possessed blue emissive sites [55,56]. From the FT-IR, XPS, TEM, UV–vis, and fluorescence emission results, we concluded that the gamma irradiation and amine passivation increased aromaticity and caused a change in physical and optical properties of the CDs, which would consequently attribute to a change in physical and electrical properties when being applied for supercapacitors.

The Raman spectra of CDs, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 were obtained, showing two peaks of the G (graphitic) and D (disordered) structures at 1590 and 1300 cm−1, respectively ( Fig. 5a) [57,58]. The intensity of the G band of CDs-200 increased compared to CDs, indicating the formation of sp2 hybridized carbon of CDs-200 after gamma irradiation, which was also in agreement with the FT-IR results. Comparing CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200, the gamma irradiation indeed increased the graphitic structure as the D band was reduced in CDs-EDA-200. Gamma irradiation possibly led to the rearrangement of carbon atoms into the graphitic structure. When comparing CDs-200 and CDs-EDA-200, the intensity of the D band of CDs-EDA-200 is higher, indicating the increase in amorphous structure of CDs-EDA-200 due to amine passivation. To study the effects of the gamma ray dose, the Raman spectra of the CDs-EDA-50 and CDs-EDA-200 were compared (Fig. 5b). There is no large difference in the ID/IG ratio because ZnO is the majority component in the composite.

The BET surface area, pore volume, and pore diameter of ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 were determined ( Table 1). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of all samples with small hysteresis loops in Fig. 6 are type-IV isotherms given by the IUPAC classification [59], arising from the characteristics of mesoporous materials [60–62]. The BET surface area of all electrodes is between 19 and 24 m2 g−1, which is typical for ZnO synthesized by the sol-gel method [63]. The pore size distributions of all electrodes are shown as inset figures. The results demonstrated that the size of all electrodes is in the mesoporous range, which agrees with the type-IV isotherm [59]. The pore volume and diameter of the composite electrodes are close to those of ZnO because only 5% carbon dots were added to the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements of the prepared electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical measurements of the prepared electrodes were performed using CV in a voltage window ranging from − 0.3–0.3 V. The CDs, CDs-50, CDs-200, CDs-EDA-50, and CDs-EDA-200 were mixed with ZnO nanoparticles to obtain ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes. The ZnO without the CDs was used as a control electrode. The CV curves of ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes measured in 6 M KOH at 5 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 7a, yielding the specific capacitances of 17.89, 25.44, 26.27, 25.42, 34.76, and 55.78 F g−1, respectively, at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs significantly improved the supercapacitor performance. Compared to the pristine ZnO electrode, the specific capacitances of ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 were elevated by 194% and 312%, emphasizing the importance of irradiation dose and amine passivation. However, the gamma irradiation alone without EDA yielded the CDs that were not greatly beneficial to the supercapacitor as compared to a combination of both gamma irradiation and amine passivation. This suggests that the EDA passivating layer produced by gamma irradiation acted as active sites for charge storage and provided favorable interface for charge transfer, which will be further examined later in the paper. Since the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode was the best, it was further tested in three different concentrations of KOH solutions as electrolytes, including 1, 3, and 6 M. The results showed that the 6 M KOH system provided the highest specific capacitance of 55.78 F g−1, while 1 and 3 M KOH solutions provided specific capacitances of 34.22 and 34.49 F g−1, respectively (Fig. 7b). This resulted from the fact that an increase in the concentration of KOH led to an enhancement in the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte [64].

To study the effects of scan rate on the specific capacitance of the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode, it was tested using CV at 5, 10, 30, 50, 70, and 100 mV s−1 (Fig. 7c). We found that the specific capacitance decreased with the increasing scan rate. This is because of the limited ion adsorption/desorption and charge transfer processes between the electrode and electrolyte at high scan rates. To obtain more electrochemical information, the GCD curves of ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 were measured at different current densities of 0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 1.2, and 2 A g−1 (Fig. 7d). As the current density was increased, the charging/discharging times became shorter for the same reason described above. The rate capability was calculated to be 70.2%. A small IR drop of about 0.07 V was observed, indicating a low series resistance of the supercapacitors. The charging time is longer than the discharging time, which is an indication of the slow pseudocapacitive behavior from the ZnO nanoparticles compared to the EDLC mechanism.

To measure the series resistance of all electrodes, the EIS technique was used. The Nyquist plots of ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 were illustrated in Fig. 7e. The intersection point on the x-axis corresponds to the intrinsic ohmic resistance of the internal resistance or equivalent series resistance (ESR) that originates from the resistance of various sources, such as electrolyte, internal resistance of electrode materials, and contact resistance between electrode and electrolyte [65]. The ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode showed the lowest ESR value of 4.21 Ω, providing the lowest internal resistance, while the ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 electrodes showed ESR values of 6.77, 6.52, 5.07, 5.98, and 4.80 Ω, respectively. The ZnO-CDs-50 gave a slightly higher specific capacitance and lower ESR value than the ZnO-CDs-200 possibly because of the higher nitrogen content as confirmed by the FT-IR and XPS results that is favorable for charge storage. This trend also agreed well with that of the specific capacitance. These results indicated that the addition of CDs that were modified by gamma irradiation and amine passivation reduced the resistance of the electrodes and suggested some favorable effects offered by the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs, which will be further examined through several electrochemical analyses below.

A long cycle life is one of the main factors for supercapacitor operation. The cycling stability was then investigated by GCD at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 7f). It can be seen that the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode retained 99.87% of the initial capacitance after being repeated for 8500 cycles, which is considered an excellent cycling stability, while the ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 electrodes retained 87.41%, 90.04%, 97.30%, 93.78%, and 99.71% of their initial capacitances, respectively. The ZnO-CDs-50 has a better cycling stability than the ZnO-CDs-200 because of its better electrochemical conductivity and charge storage due to the higher nitrogen content as discussed above. It is clear that the incorporation of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs largely improved not only the specific capacitance but also the cycling stability of the supercapacitor. This result thus confirmed the longevity of our supercapacitors for practical applications.

To examine the effects of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs on the ion transport and charge storage mechanisms of the electrodes, electrochemical analysis was performed using CV profiles with varying scan rates. Ion transport and charge storage can occur via (a) surface-reaction contribution (electric double-layer and near-surface redox reactions) and (b) diffusion-controlled processes (ion insertion/extrusion in electrode materials). The relationship between current response ( i ) and scan rate (v) was studied according to the Cottrell equation (Eq. 5) [66].

where a and b are variable parameters. The b-value can be obtained from the slope of the plot of log i vs log v ( Fig. 8a). The ideal exponent b value will have a value of 0.5 for the diffusion-controlled process and 1 for the surface reaction [67]. Clearly, the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode provided the highest b value (0.8403), followed by ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 (0.8279), ZnO-CDs (0.8237), ZnO-CDs-50 (0.8111), ZnO-CDs-200 (0.8107), and ZnO (0.7765), respectively. The b values between 0.5 and 1 indicate that both surface- and diffusion-controlled processes contributed to the ion transport and charge storage mechanisms, and the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs further enhanced the surface process, as the b values of ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 were among the highest ones. Even the unmodified CDs and gamma-irradiated CDs also increased the b values when comparing ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, and ZnO-CDs-200 to ZnO, thereby confirming that the addition of CDs boosted the surface-controlled process. To further confirm this finding, the current at 0.15 V ( i

Both Fig. 8b and c show high coefficients of determination (R2), indicating that both surface- and diffusion-controlled processes contributed to the ion transport and charge storage mechanisms, consistent with the Cottrell analysis. Although it is inconclusive to verify the more dominant process using this method, we can still ensure that the ZnO-based supercapacitors with the addition of CDs are neither purely EDLC nor Faradaic processes, but a combination of both, which is a unique characteristic of the composite electrodes combining electric double-layer and pseudocapacitve materials.

To further quantitatively determine the ratio of the capacitive contributions and the effects of CDs on the capacitive performance, the Dunn’s method was implemented. The ratio between surface and diffusion contributions can be determined from the relationship between current at a fixed voltage, i (V) and a square root of scan rate as shown in the following equations (Eqs. 9 and 10) [70,71]:

. The use of CDs-EDA-200 in the electrode provided the highest surface-controlled coefficient value, which increased from 35.86% to 57.29%, compared to pristine ZnO. This analysis demonstrated that the surface-controlled capacitance played a larger role than the diffusion-controlled capacitance after the addition of gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs in the electrodes ( Fig. 9a), which was consistent with the results obtained from the Cottrell method. Again, the unmodified CDs and gamma-irradiated CDs improved the surface-controlled process when comparing ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, and ZnO-CDs-200 to ZnO, thereby confirming that the addition of CDs boosted the surface-controlled process. Moreover, Fig. 9b shows that the higher specific capacitances can be observed at lower scan rates. This is attributed to the excellent interactions between the electrode and electrolyte at low scan rates. The CV curves in Fig. 9c and d show the comparison between the surface-controlled and diffusion-controlled contribution areas of pristine ZnO and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes. The ZnO electrode is diffusion-dominant whereas the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode is surface-dominant. This is indicative that the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs endowed some favorable surface effects to ion transport and charge storage that brought about better supercapacitor performance.

To further investigate the electrochemical performance of the electrodes, the Warburg impedance equation was used as shown in Eq. 11 [72].

is the concentration of electrolyte. It was found that the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode provided a higher Warburg coefficient (3.22 Ω s−1) than the ZnO electrode (2.98 Ω s−1) ( Fig. 10a-c). According to Eq. 12, the Warburg coefficient is inversely proportional to the square root of the diffusion coefficient. This demonstrated that the use of CDs-EDA-200 reduced the ion diffusion coefficient, which is attributed to the increase of surface-reaction capacitive contribution in the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode, as compared to the ZnO electrode.

Next, the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 and ZnO electrodes was evaluated [73]. Fig. 10d depicts that the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode has a higher ECSA (168.1 mF cm−2) than the ZnO electrode (154 mF cm−2). This clearly confirmed that the use of CDs-EDA-200 increased the electrochemical surface area, giving more electrochemically active sites for charge storage, which led to the enhancement of the specific capacitance of the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode. From all these analyses, it is conclusive that the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs promoted surface/interface phenomena that are favorable to the ion transport and charge storage processes. These surface/interface interactions between the modified CDs and ZnO possibly also provided the enhanced surface adhesion within the electrode materials and superior electrode mechanical integrity, leading to better specific capacitance and cycling stability.

To study the hydrophilic/hydrophobic effects of the prepared electrodes, the contact angles of ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes were measured using the electrolyte solution as a droplet ( Fig. 11a). The ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode showed the smallest contact angle of 29° while the ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-50 electrodes showed the contact angles of 115°, 112°, 108°, 110°, and 71°, respectively. The ZnO-CDs-50 is more hydrophilic than the ZnO-CDs-200, confirming better specific capacitance and cycling stability and lower ESR. The ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode is thus the most hydrophilic due to the high number of ammonium groups installed on the CDs surface, as confirmed by the FT-IR, XPS, and TEM characterizations. After 2 s, the electrolyte droplet was completely absorbed in the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode while it still remained on the ZnO, ZnO-CDs, ZnO-CDs-50, ZnO-CDs-200, ZnO-CDs-EDA-50, and ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrodes, showing a consistent trend of contact angles at 45°, 26°, 20°, 24°, and 11°, respectively. These results demonstrated that the CDs after modification with EDA and gamma irradiation at 200 kGy significantly increased the hydrophilicity of CDs, improving the wettability of the electrode and electrode/electrolyte interactions. The increase in hydrophilicity and wettability provided by the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs electrode enhanced the interpenetration of electrolytic ions into the pores of the electrode materials, leading to higher electrochemical performance.

Based on the results, we can conclude as follows: (1) from in the FT-IR, XPS, Raman, and UV–vis results, the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs provided higher amount of π electrons and aromaticity in the structure that raised the number of transferred electron and electron mobility for charge transport, (2) from electrochemical analyses, they facilitated the surface-controlled process for charge storage and improved the surface-controlled capacitance, and (3) from contact angle measurement, the alteration of surface chemistry by gamma irradiation and amine passivation of the CDs resulted in higher hydrophilicity and better surface wettability allowing good interactions between electrode and electrolyte (Fig. 11b). (4) The graphitic carbon formed by gamma irradiation and defective structure generated by EDA can contribute to the electrochemical performance via providing better charge transport and electrically active sites for charge storage, respectively. The enhanced surface properties also suggested enhanced interactions within the electrode materials that led to better material adhesion. All of these, therefore, synergistically gave rise to the favorable surface and interface between electrode and electrolyte, giving more electrochemically available sites to reduce the resistance and enhance the specific capacitance and cycling stability of the supercapacitors. This emphasizes that not only an improved specific capacitance but also enhanced electrochemical stability and mechanical integrity were provided by the gamma-irradiated/amine-passivated carbon dots to the ZnO-based electrodes.

There are some previous reports on the fabrication and characterization of supercapacitors based on ZnO nanoparticles. Fang et al. reported a specific capacitance as high as 786 F g−1, but their capacitance retention dropped to 92% after only 500 cycles [35], which is much inferior to our supercapacitors (99.87% after 8500 cycles) ( Table 2). Similarly, Dhivya et al. obtained a high capacitance of 518 F g−1 [74], but the cycling stability was still inferior to ours. Although the specific capacitance of our supercapacitors was not the best, compared to some previous reports, our supercapacitors are among the best in terms of cycling stability. Based on our work, we have demonstrated that the gamma-irradiated, amine-passivated CDs can effectively enhance both specific capacitance by more than three times and cycling stability. The CDs will definitely improve the electrochemical performance of other metal oxide-based supercapacitors by offering positively synergistic properties, as proved in this work.

In summary, the CDs derived from polyurethane were successfully modified by gamma irradiation and amine passivation. We demonstrated that not only the CDs but also a combination of gamma irradiation and amine passivation helped significantly improve the supercapacitor performance. Signifying the impacts of irradiation dose, the CDs-EDA-200 gave the best specific capacitance and cycling stability by improving various key factors, including the surface-controlled capacitance, series resistance, ion diffusion coefficient, electrochemical active surface area, wettability, and mechanical adhesion of the electrode. Compared to the pure ZnO electrode, the ZnO-CDs-EDA-200 electrode boosted the specific capacitance by 312% and the cycling stability from 87.41% to 99.87% after 8500 cycles. Based on this work, a combination of gamma irradiation and amine passivation proved to be a powerful strategy in preparing the CDs that enhanced the performance of ZnO-based supercapacitors and will provide the key basis for the implementation to metal oxide-based supercapacitors or other electrochemical devices in future work.

Peerasak Paoprasert: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.
